sentences.text: So to continue, I didn't want to speak to him, and he wanted to know why.
And I said, because you shoved this old man for no reason at all.
And so he just shrugged it off, and he left.
That was when we were lined up in the street, in the town, before we went to the train station.
And then, when we got to the train, we were lucky if you can call it that.
We were lucky, because we got on first.
And so my father took a corner in the wagon.
And we were sort of leaning against the wall.
Because we found out later how lucky that was, because they packed the train so full.
I don't even know if some of the people were able to sit down all the time.
And they were just standing in the middle of the wagon.
And we were able to sort of sit back and lean against the wall, so that was, you know, a really fortunate thing for us.
And my recollection then was, of course I was sitting next to my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's like I can-- I can see it in my mind, you know.
But I can't recall that many things happening.
I remember my father looking into a Hebrew book all three days that he was travelling.
I don't recall if he ever opened his mouth, even.
My mother was sitting there saying that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] OK.
She kept saying [SOBBING] that God should save the children.
[CRYING] She never even mentioned herself, I guess.
She had some gut feeling that-- where we're going.
And maybe she heard enough, or maybe she could see the situation.
So I remember her just saying that God should watch over us.
And then-- OK-- I recall that at some stops, we would-- I would lean out the window and get some water.
They would come to the station and give us water.
I can't recall what I ate.
I can't recall whether I ate.
I remember some-- some noises, some people bickering a little.
But my mother wasn't the type to get involved with any of that.
And so we were just huddled together.
And--
So I was going to say, besides my mother and father, it was only the three younger girls.
My two brothers and sister were somewhere else and not taken in the same train with us.
No.
No.
Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my younger brother, the younger one of the two, was married.
And he was in another town.
He was eventually taken from there, to Auschwitz.
My older sister was living and married in another town.
And so she [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was taken from there.
And my older brother was somewhere, wherever he happened to be in another town.
And so it was only my parents and the three girls that were together.
And then I remember the scene very clearly when we arrived.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a lot of shouting by the Germans and very-- you know, screaming and shouting and giving orders.
It was day.
I think it was afternoon.
And the trains emptied.
And there was, like, masses and masses of people.
I mean, you could see just like, you know, at a rally, or-- I don't know how to describe-- just masses of people.
And instantly, as we got off, they separated the men from the women.
And so we clung together, and they lined us up.
And as we walked forward, I remember looking back, and I saw-- you know, being that it was a very Hasidic town, and a lot of very religious Jews, mostly wore beards and black hats.
And so, when I looked back to see my father, I could never make out who-- anymore where he is or-- I never saw him again once we got off the train.
Never had a chance to say a word to him or him to me.
Because when the doors slid open, they started screeching.
And you know, you felt panicky and scared.
And then we walked towards this famous Dr.
Mengele, who was sitting at a table with some others.
As I recall, he wasn't alone.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my two sisters were on the right side of my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was on the left side of my mother, holding her arm as usual.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I could see, you know, that each person or family that approached that table-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that there is a separation.
But I don't know.
I always say it was an angel who take care of me.
Because I really wasn't going to let go of my mother.
Because wherever she went, I wanted to go.
But there were just one more row of people in front of us.
It would've been our turn next.
I pulled my hand out from my mother.
I ran in between my two sisters, and I took them.
And literally a second later, Mengele said, the three of you go right and for my mother to go left.
So it just took that instant.
You know, I certainly don't think I was smart enough to figure that out myself.
But how-- why I did it, and-- and-- I guess it was meant to be.
So this is how I managed to stay alive.
And, of course, that was the last time we saw our mother.
I think that what is sad is also that we never said a word to each other.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because there was so much shouting and such terror that you just went.
But I think that the moment we went to the right, we knew somehow that it's better to go to the right than to the left.
So that was it.
And then we arrived to Auschwitz.
Right away, they took us-- only woman.
We were never together with men.
They took us into these huge rooms, told us to undress.
And we took a shower.
They shaved our heads.
And they gave us these striped dresses, this uniform.
And-- and we have arrived.
So then it was getting dark.
And I remember coming into this room through a glass door.
I looked in, and I saw a lot of people with striped dresses.
And everybody was shaved.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And for that moment, I didn't realize that I look exactly the same.
And I said to my sister, you know, it looks like death rooms full of men.
And they look to me like prisoners.
Prisoners, you know, that I  know look like that and until the realization hit me that this is me.
It's the same thing.
And then, you know, they gave us this bunk.
We were there about, I think, 12  or 14 people.
And I'm sure that the whole world has seen the bunks in-- the wooden bunks in Auschwitz.
Yes.
And I don't even know how-- I'm thinking now how it was possible to lay down, unless we were laying on top of each other and-- when you had to sleep.
Because I can't see where anybody-- I mean, there was certainly no room to stretch out for that many people.
But I do remember that I was sitting on the edge of the bunk facing the aisle.
And this was the very first day.
And a very pretty young woman-- and being pretty in Auschwitz wasn't easy-- she had curly hair.
She was petite, very pretty woman.
Turns out she was a Jewish Polish woman.
She came over to us.
And she held a batch of postcards.
And she said, who would like to write home?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I said, me.
Because, you know, I mean, I didn't connect yet.
I just, you know, didn't-- totally didn't realize what happened and-- and that there is nobody to write to.
And I remember doing this and saying, I would like to.
And she slapped me across the face with such force.
Now, you have to remember, I was never slapped.
I was never hit.
I was-- you know, before I went to school, they used to go out to get me a fresh roll.
Because I didn't want to eat one that was sitting there from yesterday.
I-- you know.
And like I said, justice meant a lot to me always, you know.
And when she hit me for no reason whatsoever, I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was stunned at first.
And I didn't say a word.
But I made up my mind.
I made up my mind.
And I made a silent vow.
As long as I'm going to be in Germany, nobody will ever lay a hand on me again.
Nobody ever did.
Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, she's asking me.
She's offering.
And I'm just answering very nicely.
And if this is how it's going to be, I will make sure that I'm smart enough not to get hit again.
And so I was a very good girl.
I worked very hard in labor camp and everywhere.
And they liked me very much.
And I was never hit again and-- if this is any consolation.
Yes.
She, I understand, later told everybody she was very mean.
She was very bitter.
And for a very long time, I thought that she was a very low-life of a person that she could, you know, treat people that have arrived and so on.
But maybe you can [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] forgive a little bit of her action, when you think that, from what she told people, that she was there since 1940 or '41.
And she said to us Hungarian girls, she said, well, you were sleeping on silk pillows while we were digging graves for our parents, to bury our parents.
So I guess she had reason to be very bitter and angry.
But nevertheless, I didn't feel it was justified.
So like I said, I-- so that was my first initiation in Auschwitz.
And then we were there for six weeks.
And they started to talk of selecting some girls, because they need to take them to another city, to a labor camp.
Let me just ask you-- while you were there for the six weeks, did you work, at that point?
No, not at all.
It was just the routine of-- to back track, just for a minute. --is was routine.
You got up-- I don't know-- 6 o'clock in the morning.
You stood on line.
And they counted and counted and counted.
It was cold.
It was hot.
It was freezing and raining.
It didn't matter to them.
It was like, an absolute obsession with them.
Because very few people ever escaped, even those that were very brave to try it.
So I don't know.
But they did-- I guess now-- as a matter of fact, while I'm talking about it, they may have been very worried for the world to find out what is going on in Auschwitz.
So they want to make sure nobody escapes and tells the story, even though one or two or three or some did.
How, I don't know.
And so we, um-- we were counted.
It was called the [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
You were standing in line.
And-- and then you were given some-- who knows what you were given to
Eat?
I remember they gave bread.
The portion of bread, it was like a brick.
And it was very hard.
And for three days I absolutely had no-- not a crumb in my mouth.
Because I was very fussy and very bad eater, and so I wouldn't touch it.
And I was joking, afterwards, that later I couldn't get enough of it.
Because, you know, it was as good as cake.
And then they came around with this cauldron of soup that barely had anything in it, you know.
And they would dish it out.
And-- and so really it was, for me, just this dreary-- day after day.
But I do remember, though, one incident which is enough to give you nightmares-- give me nightmares for the rest of my life-- is that I got up, one day-- one night, in the middle of the night.
It was very dark.
And I got out of the bunk.
And I went outside, to the bathroom, which was a very makeshift kind of a thing.
And I was standing all alone there.
And it was very quiet.
And I saw this huge, huge chimney.
And the flames were just going out of it like-- and I was standing there mesmerized.
I mean, I remember the very thoughts that I thought at the time.
I said, my god, these flames are reaching the heavens.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't know what it was, until later.
That was still when I was quite new.
Actually, the whole time I was in Auschwitz I didn't know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know so much.
It was a short time that we were there.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
Maybe some of the older people knew.
I know that somebody was saying that, like, we had a neighbor at home who [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went there with her mother.
And-- and somebody came with a story that this girl went crazy.
She was separated from her mother.
She was, I remember, supposed to be an old maid.
She might have been in her late 20s.
And-- and they brought back some horror stories now and then.
How clear it was to most of my peers, I don't know-- what was happening.
I do know that, by the time I left, I certainly knew that there isn't much of a chance that the parents and the children and the elderly-- that they live.
I found out through the Israeli films a lot more than I knew at the time, watching them later, and people from Poland, and other people speaking about it.
But there was a lady who was very well known.
She later lived in Israel, a very wonderful lady.
And my sister spoke to her.
And she said, well, what do you think?
They want to select people to go take us away.
And you knew enough that you would were afraid.
Where are they going to take you?
So I guess there was already a fear that-- and we knew enough that they can take you away any day in your life.
So she said, if there is a selection of people to go, make sure you get out of here.
Make sure you get out of here.
Don't worry, just try to be part of the group that's going out.
Because nothing's going to be worse than Auschwitz.
And so they lined us up-- a single file.
We walked.
This Mengele was standing there.
And I remember a huge woman, a German woman in a uniform.
She was standing next to him.
And I was twitching back and forth.
Should I stand in front of my sister, back of my sister, in the middle of my-- between the two?
Because I knew that I'm small.
I'm very-- very little.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was afraid that they were going to just pick me out.
Like, I got away with it once, but will I do it again?
You know?
I just didn't want them to look at me.
So-- but I had no choice.
So we were dressed at the time.
And we were standing single file.
And I said, well, if I'm going to stand-- one sister was very skinny, and one was heavier.
And they both looked quite more mature than I.
So where will I show up the worst, you know, if I'm in the front or whatever?
Anyhow, my sister, she happens to live now in Toronto with her family.
She looked very healthy.
Turned out, later on, that she was weaker than I was and could tolerate less what she went through.
But she always-- you know, they always put her through, because she looked strong.
And my other sister was tall and slim.
And they always rejected her.
She didn't look strong enough.
And the truth is that she really wasn't.
She wasn't, but they could see it.
Then it was my turn.
And this heavyset woman says, right away-- again, it was left and right.
It was obviously left or right.
You stay here, or you-- or you go with the transport to Gelsenkirchen.
And this heavyset woman-- you go to the left, which meant-- you know, she just barely looked at me.
You go to the left.
She dismissed me.
And he stopped her.
And he said, no, she's going to the right.
Yeah.
Now, this is-- I mean, another miracle that I can't figure out-- what he saw in me and what made him do it.
And, again, whether my resemblance to a German kid, whether he-- he actually had a heart, that he wanted to send me out.
But I doubt it very much.
It was just-- maybe he just wanted to contradict her.
I don't know.
But-- so I remember my sister and I being very happy that we were chosen.
We were convinced, at that point, it's good to get away from Auschwitz.
One.
The other one was rejected-- the one that I'm telling you was oldest of the three.
She was slim and didn't look as strong to them, so she was rejected.
And we were very unhappy about it, to be split up again.
So they let us stay over.
We-- we slept overnight in the bunk.
And then the following day we were supposed to leave.
And my sister, the middle one, with somebody's help, there, tried to smuggle in my older sister.
And she accomplished it.
They smuggled her out from wherever she was.
And they brought-- she brought-- OK.
Now, the next morning, they, uh-- we went through the same thing, but undressed, completely undressed.
And I remember them being so particular that if you had the slightest little scratch, you know, they would, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they would not let you go.
They were like-- you have to be 1,000% healthy and-- and clean-looking and whatever.
So we went through, the three of us.
And I remember seeing somebody who had a little blemish on their body.
And they rejected her.
And at that point, I must have gotten-- in the back of my buttocks, I had a little pimple which didn't mean a thing.
But I was terrified that they are going to now make me-- they'll-- they'll reject me.
So when I saw that they did for very little reason, my sister went.
So one of them was rejected once again.
The one-- The same one?
The same one.
The other one, they let go.
And I didn't wait for them to tell me what to do.
And I just ran over.
I just didn't wait.
The moment my sister went, I just ran.
And I figured if they don't like it, they'll take me back.
But they let me go.
They didn't pull me back.
Because I was afraid that if I'm going to start, they'll look me up in the front and the back.
They'll see a tiny little pimple.
And they will reject me.
So I didn't want to give them a chance to look too much.
And so that was the last time, at that point.
She did survive, my sister.
But she wound up in-- in Bergen-Belsen, which was very terrible.
And she was very, very sick towards the end of the war.
She went through hell.
And-- but my sister and I did go to-- to the city, beautiful city.
And it's called-- in the Ruhrgebiet-- Gelsenkirchen.
And--
So now you-- the one sister, the one-- the thin one--
Yeah.
--she-- she got rejected.
And so she stayed in Auschwitz and was later transferred.
Yes.
Right.
Yes.
Same type of train.
Same type of train.
As a matter of fact, I think that I have a problem with an allergy, today, because of that.
I think so, possibly not.
But what happened was we traveled overnight.
I don't recall exactly how long the trip took.
But I remember everybody laid down, and we were very tired.
And as I was sleeping, somebody's boot hit me right here in the bridge of my nose.
And I recall just absolutely feeling that it's-- I can faint from it.
And then in the morning, I saw that the skin was torn, and it was very painful.
I never found out who did it.
And, of course, you couldn't blame anybody.
They didn't-- were not aware, you know, of doing it.
But I had a mark here for a very long time.
And we arrived to the city.
And they had-- like, in an open field, they had set up tents, not barracks like they had there.
It was tents and a whole different life started for me.
They were much more tolerant.
I mean, we-- the-- we had the rules, of course.
And we had-- we had a fence.
You couldn't just come and go.
You-- you were fenced in in barbed wire.
And you had the tower that looked down and made sure.
But the barbed wire was not electrified.
So that if somebody really made an effort, you could pry it apart-- which I found out later at one point that you can do that.
And what they did-- they give us much more food.
They talked to us much better.
You didn't feel that you were threatened by death every moment of the day.
Right.
And they had a factory, a munition factory, a large munition factory.
They had tracks right outside of the tents that took trains where, I suppose, they, you know, picked up the ammunition.
And there we were introduced to this factory.
We had to work 12 hours a day or 12 hours a night.
One week it was daytime, one week it was in the nighttime.
And so we went to work, like I said.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That slap in Auschwitz taught me that I am not trying to get out of anything, whatever I have to do, as long as I'm there.
And somehow, I didn't know how and when, but you did have that feeling that it's a matter of time, and that I'll be free.
In other words, I was never despondent to the point where I said, well, I'm going to die tomorrow.
I just-- somehow, when you're that young, I guess, you have the confidence that you're going to make it.
That was in the summer.
Because we arrived to Auschwitz sometimes in May, and that was six weeks later.
It was not easy.
It was these-- you know, like you can visualize today, the slot machines, you know, where they have this arm?
You had to keep pulling this arm down.
And it was very hot.
And the steel pieces, you know, had to-- it made a hole or whatever other shapes that you made.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was-- it was very, very difficult.
At 12 o'clock at night, they gave us half an hour for eating lunch.
And I remember laying down on the floor and rather sleeping than eating.
And at one point, I remember, a German came over.
And he just kicked me with  a foot that it's time to get up.
I mean, not kick me hard, but just letting me know it's time to get up and-- and go to work.
I had a few experiences there that were interesting.
One woman-- a German woman-- I can't recall the first meeting in camp.
But she took a liking to me.
And she would come to visit me every time she had a day off.
And she would bring her friends.
And I cannot figure out whether she might have had a child that she missed or lost or what-- what her background was.
But she used to call me [NON-ENGLISH] and come to visit me and ask me if I'm comfortable.
She would just lift me up and put me on a table and sit there.
And I remember whenever she came, mostly Sundays, my friends would say, ah, your woman is coming to visit, you know.
And one time it was starting to get, in the fall, very cold.
And they handed us coats, you know, all kinds of coats that they probably took from Auschwitz, from people.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she came and asked me if I liked the coat.
And, of course, I didn't like the coat.
You know, that wasn't what I had in mind.
So she took me into the-- to the warehouse where they had these piles of coats.
And she walked around with me-- I should pick out one the I like better.
She didn't.
She didn't.
She might have come one time to visit.
She may have worked nearby.
She was in uniform.
She was in uniform.
So-- but I didn't see her.
She didn't live in the camp.
That I know for a fact.
